function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts), 'close' (close stdin/send EOF).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
For servers/watchers, do NOT use shell-level background wrappers (nohup/disown/setsid/trailing '&') in foreground mode. Use background=true so Hermes can track lifecycle and output.
After starting a server, verify readiness with a health check or log signal, then run tests in a separate terminal() call. Avoid blind sleep loops.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180, foreground max: 600). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily. Foreground timeout above 600s is rejected; use background=true for longer commands.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified exactly once when the process finishes. **This is the right choice for almost every long-running task** — tests, builds, deployments, multi-item batch jobs, anything that takes over a minute and has a defined end. Use this and keep working on other things; the system notifies you on exit. MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE with watch_patterns — when both are set, watch_patterns is dropped.

watch_patterns.description: Strings to watch for in background process output. HARD RATE LIMIT: at most 1 notification per 15 seconds per process — matches arriving inside the cooldown are dropped. After 3 consecutive 15-second windows with dropped matches, watch_patterns is automatically disabled for that process and promoted to notify_on_complete behavior (one notification on exit, no more mid-process spam). USE ONLY for truly rare, one-shot mid-process signals on LONG-LIVED processes that will never exit on their own — e.g. ['Application startup complete'] on a server so you know when to hit its endpoint, or ['migration done'] on a daemon. DO NOT use for: (1) end-of-run markers like 'DONE'/'PASS' — use notify_on_complete instead; (2) error patterns like 'ERROR'/'Traceback' in loops or multi-item batch jobs — they fire on every iteration and you'll hit the strike limit fast; (3) anything you'd ever combine with notify_on_complete. When in doubt, choose notify_on_complete. MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE with notify_on_complete — set one, not both.

# Video 6: 10 Hermes Agent Features Most People Aren't Using
## By Sharbel A. | Video ID: fLmlXXz5MO4

---

## Overview
Most people install Hermes and use it like a smarter ChatGPT — wire up Telegram, point at a model, ask questions, close window. This misses most of what Hermes can actually do. Hermes is an **agent layer** that behaves more like **infrastructure** than a normal chat app.

---

## The 10 Features

### 1. **soul.md — Persistent Personality & Operating Style**
- Hermes can have a **persistent operating style**, not just a one-off system prompt
- Create a `soul.md` file that defines your agent's personality and behavior
- Gives your assistant a consistent identity across sessions
- **Setup:** Create a `soul.md` file in your Hermes configuration directory

---

### 2. **Session Search — Search Past Conversations**
- Hermes can **search past sessions** when you say "We talked about this before"
- Ask it to search and summarize previous sessions
- **Usage:** "Search our past sessions for [topic]. Summarize what we learned."
- Saves you from repeating yourself; every serious operator needs this

---

### 3. **Skills as Workflows — Reusable Playbooks**
- Skills are **reusable workflows** — turn complex processes into callable skills
- Instead of rewriting complex prompts every time, call a skill
- Examples: Nova YouTube agent, Sage X (X/ Twitter agent)
- Both are open source and available on Sharbel's previous videos
- **Usage:** "Show me the skills you have available." Then invoke by name
- **Setup:** Create skills as structured workflow files in your Hermes skills directory

---

### 4. **Telegram Gateway as Control Surface**
- Hermes + Telegram = real tools, not just a chatbot
- Through Telegram you get: tools, persistent memory, file system access, scheduled jobs, workflows
- Normal Telegram chatbots only reply; Hermes Telegram retains the full agent layer
- **Key point:** Telegram is just the interface, the magic is the agent behind it
- Can run across different platforms if needed

---

### 5. **Cron Jobs — Recurring Automated Work**
- Hermes can do **recurring work without manual prompting**
- Schedule jobs: daily AI briefs, daily opportunity scans, hourly checks
- Output goes to Telegram or another delivery surface
- **Usage:** "Show me my active scheduled jobs"
- This is where Hermes stops being reactive — it runs while you're not watching
- **Setup:** Configure cron jobs through Hermes; define schedule and workflow

---

### 6. **Sub-Agents — Delegation to Isolated Agents**
- Hermes can **delegate work to isolated agents** and only return the final summary
- Useful for complex tasks that would be overwhelming in one thread
- Each sub-agent is isolated and focused on its specific task
- **Usage:** "Use sub-agent to help with [task]"
- Keeps your main thread clean while background work happens

---

### 7. **Profiles — Multiple Isolated Agent Identities**
- Run **multiple isolated agent identities** with separate:
  - Configurations
  - Memories
  - Sessions
  - Skills
  - Cron jobs
- Example use case: separate agents for personal work, coding, research, content, operations
- Your coding agent doesn't need your content calendar; your content agent doesn't need production credentials
- **Note:** Profiles are separate workspaces, not security sandboxes — don't confuse them with blocked access
- **Setup:** Configure multiple profiles in Hermes settings

---

### 8. **Model & Provider Switching — Provider Agnostic**
- Hermes is **not locked to one model provider**
- Can use multiple providers simultaneously: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, Open Router, local models
- **Separates agent layer from model layer** — the model is just the brain you plug in
- Use different models for different tasks (e.g., one for coding, cheaper one for research)
- **Setup:** Configure multiple providers in Hermes settings

---

### 9. **/btw — Side Question Mode (Slash By The Way)**
- Ask **quick questions without derailing the main task**
- Use `/btw` command when you need to ask something adjacent without interrupting the workflow
- **Problem it solves:** Most people either interrupt the current task OR open a new chat and lose context
- **Usage:** `/btw [your quick question]`
- Agent equivalent of "quick question, but don't stop what you're doing"
- Keeps main thread context clean while getting quick answers

---

### 10. **Hooks, boot.md & Event Automation**
- **Hooks:** "When X happens, run Y" — automation triggers around Hermes gateway life cycle
- **boot.md:** Startup checklist that runs when gateway starts
- **Example boot.md use case:** 
  - Check if gateway came back cleanly
  - Check if cron jobs failed overnight
  - Check logs for errors
  - Summarize anything needing attention
  - Otherwise stay silent
- This is where Hermes becomes **infrastructure**, not just an app
- Hooks let you wire Hermes into the **life cycle of the agent itself**
- Most people think of agents as chat; hooks are when agents start becoming infrastructure

---

## Key Takeaway
These 10 features transform Hermes from a "smarter chatbot" into an **agent infrastructure platform**:
- Persistent personality (soul.md)
- Memory across sessions (session search)
- Reusable workflows (skills)
- Real control surface (Telegram gateway)
- Background automation (cron jobs)
- Parallel processing (sub-agents)
- Multi-agent orchestration (profiles)
- Provider flexibility (model switching)
- Context-preserving quick questions (/btw)
- Event-driven automation (hooks/boot.md)

Most people only scratch the surface. The real system is much bigger.
EOF
echo "Summary created successfully""}
